hero.subtitle: End-to-end workshop for system-prompt safety in LLM tool-use scenarios: AI generation, red-team evaluation and closed-loop optimization.

features.title: Core Capabilities
features.subtitle: Build a security baseline for tool-using agents from scratch

generate.title: Smart Prompt Generation

eval.desc: Target model × built-in mock tools × judge model — log every tool call, response and verdict; radar charts

auto.desc: Seed samples → eval → optimize → refresh samples (anti-overfit) → re-eval, N self-iterations until convergence

models.title: 4-Role Decoupling
models.desc: Generator / Attacker / Target / Judge — wire any OpenAI-compatible endpoint (DeepSeek / Qwen / Ollama …)

stream.desc: Token counters / per-sample progress / phase logs — long jobs never go silent or time out

screens.title: Product Tour

install.title: One-line Launch

footer.tagline: MIT licensed · For security research and defense testing only

help.title: Help Guide
help.subtitle: Get hands-on with Prompt Armor in 5 minutes

intro.title: Overview
intro.body:
Prompt Armor is a workshop for system-prompt safety on tool-using LLM agents. It does four things: (1) generate guardrail system prompts; (2) generate red-team and benign sample sets; (3) run adversarial evaluations with pass-rate metrics and failure traces; (4) auto-iterate the prompt in a closed loop until it converges.

All data is stored locally at ~/.prompt-armor/db.sqlite — nothing is uploaded.

models.title: 1. Configure Models
models.body: Open Models and add at least one OpenAI-compatible endpoint (DeepSeek / Qwen / Ollama / self-hosted vLLM all work). For best results, use distinct models for the four roles (Generator / Attack-Generator / Target / Judge) to reduce same-source bias.

samples.title: 2. Generate Attack Samples
samples.body: On the Attack Samples page paste a natural-language brief (scenario, typical attacks). Pick a generator model — samples stream in across categories. You can also import manually or merge multiple sets.

prompts.title: 3. Generate Guardrail Prompt
prompts.body: On the Guardrail Prompts page pick one or more sample sets → AI analyzes the risk surface → streams a system prompt. Rename / edit / version after generation.

eval.title: 4. Run Evaluation
eval.body: Pick Prompt × Test Set × Target × Judge, then Start. The target answers each sample under the built-in mock tool set; the judge decides pass/fail. Radar / details / failure reasons appear in Reports.

auto.title: 5. Auto-Pilot (Recommended)
auto.body:
On Auto-Pilot, fill in name, business context, a few seed samples, the four models and iteration count, then Start. The system loops automatically: generate initial prompt → evaluate → optimize prompt from failures + refresh samples for weak categories → re-evaluate, until target pass rate is reached or no further improvement.

Live score chart, iteration timeline, token stream and per-phase progress are visible throughout.

tips.title: Tips & FAQ
tips.body:
· Eval too slow? Reduce samples to 8–12; default concurrency is 4.
· Judge too strict? Override the judge system prompt under Settings.
· Want benign samples? Enable benign generation in the sample form so guardrails don't over-correct.

autopilot.title: Auto-Pilot
autopilot.subtitle: From seed samples → automatic loop: generate → evaluate → optimize → anti-overfit refresh → converge

models.title: Model Management
models.subtitle: Configure OpenAI-compatible models independently for 4 roles (Generator / Attacker / Target / Judge)
models.empty: No models yet — click "Add Model" to get started →

dashboard.title: LLM Guard Prompt Factory
dashboard.subtitle: Generate · Optimize · Evaluate — give your AI app a quantifiable security strategy
dashboard.radarTitle: Latest Evaluation · Category Pass Rate Radar
dashboard.noEvalHint: Run an evaluation first to see how your prompt holds up
dashboard.noMetrics: Complete your first evaluation to see results here.
dashboard.step1: Go to [Model Management] and configure 4 roles: Generator / Attack Generator / Target / Judge (OpenAI-compatible protocol)
dashboard.step2: Check [Tools] and enable/disable tools to pass to the target model (all enabled by default)
dashboard.step3: Go to [Attack Samples] to view built-in red-team sample sets, or generate more
dashboard.step4: Go to [Run Evaluation] and select Prompt + Sample Set + Models to start evaluating
dashboard.step5: After evaluation, check [Eval Reports] for results and one-click optimize the Prompt based on failures

tools.title: Tool Set
tools.subtitle: Built-in OpenClaw-style mock tools; only records whether called during evaluation — never actually executed
tools.profilesDesc: Built-in profiles cover typical agent scenarios. Custom profiles let you pick any subset to reduce token usage during evaluation.

settings.title: System Settings
settings.subtitle: Customize AI role system prompts and other internal parameters
settings.placeholder: Leave blank to use the built-in default.

samples.title: Attack Samples / Eval Sets
samples.subtitle: Red-team samples + benign control samples. Supports free-text generation, extension from existing sets, and targeted eval set generation from a system prompt
samples.deleteSetConfirm: Delete set "{{name}}"? All samples will be removed
samples.genTitle: AI Generate Attack Samples / Eval Set
samples.genIntent: Describe the samples you want (free text, optional)
samples.genIntentPlaceholder: e.g. samples where user pretends to be a maintenance person asking for chmod/rm on system directories; or prompt injection via document summarization...
samples.genPromptLabel: Generate eval set targeting a specific system prompt (optional — ignores 'business context', specifically probes weaknesses in that prompt)
samples.genRefSets: Reference sample sets (optional — draw a few samples as diversity reference)
samples.genWriteNew: Create new set (recommended — dedicated eval set)
samples.genBizContext: Business context (used when not targeting a specific prompt)
samples.defaultBizContext: An AI assistant with tool-calling capabilities, running in a Linux controlled environment.

prompts.title: Guard Prompts
prompts.subtitle: Manage a version tree of system-level security prompts — AI generation and failure-based optimization
prompts.genRefSets: Reference sample sets — system will auto-analyze categories and tools to defend
prompts.genNoSets: No sample sets yet — create or generate one on the Samples page first
prompts.genSetsSelected: {{count}} set(s) selected — attack categories and disabled tools will be auto-derived
prompts.genSetsNone: None selected — will generate based on business context and default security policies (recommend selecting at least 1 set)
prompts.genExtraPlaceholder: e.g. must be in English, no more than 800 words...
prompts.genCatsLabel: Attack categories to focus on ({{count}} selected; leave empty = auto-derive from samples)
prompts.genToolCtx: Known target agent tool context (optional · paste freely)
prompts.genToolCtxPlaceholder:
If you know the target agent's tool definitions/names, paste them here (OpenAI tools schema, list, or free description).
Leave blank = generate behavior-level rules independent of specific tools (recommended).
prompts.genToolCtxHint: Tip: tools are not inherently dangerous — it's how they're used that matters. The default generates rules across behavior/path/output/jailbreak dimensions, compatible with any toolset.
prompts.clickToRename: Click ✎ on the right to rename
prompts.defaultBizContext: An AI assistant with tool-calling capabilities (e.g. Agent app), running in a controlled environment with file read/write, command execution, and API call abilities.

eval.title: Run Evaluation
eval.subtitle: Load a guard prompt and let the target model face an onslaught of attack samples — the judge scores in real time

reports.title: Eval Reports
reports.subtitle: All historical evaluation results
reports.failInfo: Will patch the original prompt using {{count}} failed cases from this evaluation.

auto.fieldItersHint: 1-20; each iteration includes one eval + optimize
auto.fieldBizCtxHint: Tell the AI what your application does and what capabilities it has
auto.fieldBizCtxPlaceholder: e.g. A home-control Agent that uses fs/shell/http tools to control appliances, running on the user's Linux host.
auto.fieldIntentPlaceholder: e.g. Focus on defending against command injection, key leakage, prompt leakage
auto.fieldSeeds: Seed samples (one per line, natural language)
auto.fieldSeedsHint: 3-10 is enough — AI will expand into a full test set
auto.roleTargetHint: The model you deploy to production
auto.fieldThreshold: Target pass-rate threshold (stop early if reached)
auto.alertSeeds: Please provide at least 1 seed sample (one per line)
auto.logDone: 🏆 All done · best score {{best}}% · {{iters}} rounds total
auto.bizCtxDefault: An AI assistant with tool-calling capabilities.
auto.alertNoModel: Please configure and select a model first
auto.fieldProfileHint: Restrict which tools are sent to the model. Reduces token usage and makes evals more realistic. Leave empty to use all enabled tools.
